summary:
Nea Nikomedeia is a Neolithic settlement site in the Emathia regional unit of Macedonia in northern Greece, dated to approximately 6230 to 5500 BCE and excavated by Robert Rodden in the 1960s. It is one of the earliest known Neolithic settlements in Greece outside Thessaly and provides important evidence for the speed and geographic extent of the early farming expansion into southeastern Europe following its initial establishment in Greek Thessaly by communities like those of the [[event:1416|Sesklo culture]].

The site features rectangular mud-brick buildings with wattle-and-daub walls, organized in a planned settlement layout. Inhabitants cultivated emmer wheat, einkorn, and lentils and kept domesticated sheep, goats, and cattle. Among the significant finds from the site are clay figurines including a large seated female figure, clay vessels with geometric painted decoration, and evidence of a possible communal building or shrine containing unusual clay objects and figurines, suggesting a degree of ritual organization at the community level.

Nea Nikomedeia represents the early establishment of farming communities in northern Greece and the southern Balkans, contributing to the chain of Neolithic expansion that would eventually bring agriculture across Europe. Its excavation was significant methodologically as one of the first applications of modern systematic sampling techniques to a Neolithic site in the Balkans.
